coredata.dc:title: Single-step synthesis of highly photoluminescent carbon dots for rapid detection of Hg2+ with excellent sensitivity
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Colloid and Interface Science
coredata.dc:creator: Wang, Bei-Bei
Jin, Jian-Cheng
Xu, Zi-Qiang
Jiang, Zi-Wen
Li, Xun
Jiang, Feng-Lei
Liu, Yi
coredata.dc:description: Carbon dots (C-dots) are superior in the aspects of excellent water solubility, good biocompatibility, environmentally friendliness and non-blinking fluorescence. In this work, highly photoluminescent small-size C-dots (QY = 18.8%, quinine sulfate as standard) with narrow size distribution (1.70 ± 0.21 nm) have been synthesized by using citric acid and triethylamine through hydrothermal method. The optimal excitation and emission wavelength of C-dots are 350 nm and 437 nm, respectively. And the prepared C-dots display excitation-independent behavior due to less surface defects and uniform size. Interestingly, the fluorescence of C-dots could be rapidly and selectively quenched by Hg2+ within 200 s at room temperature without further modification. Under optimal conditions, the limit of detection (LOD) was measured to be nanomolar level (2.8 nM) with a linear range of 0.05–7 μM, lower than the previous published reports. Furthermore, our results reveal that static quenching mechanism was dominated in the process in which Hg2+ coordinate with the oxygen-containing groups of C-dots to form nonfluorescent complexes. And only the addition of Hg2+ destroyed the surface defects of C-dots resulting in the fluorescent quenching. The presence of other common interfering metal ions reported in previous literature (Ag+, Cu2+, Fe3+) do not affect the surface defects, which has rarely been reported before. Besides, this sensing platform has been further successfully applied to the label free detection of Hg2+ in tap water and living cells. These conclusions demonstrate the great potential of our C-dots in selective detection of environmental and cellular Hg2+, which may achieve a lot of achievements in clinical diagnosis and other biological researches.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Nitrogen-doped
Carbon dots
Hg2+ detection
Cell imaging

xocs:meta.xocs:srctitle: Journal of Colloid and Interface Science

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Sensitive detection of Hg2+ in tap water samples
Assessment of cell viability and fluorescence imaging
Application of C-dots for cellular Hg2+ detection

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Optimization of reaction conditions: (a) Influence of reaction temperature and ratio of CA/TEA on the FL intensity of C-dots. (b) FL emission spectra of C-dots with different ratios of CA/TEA at 160 °C. (c) FL emission spectra of C-dots with a ratio of CA/TEA = 1:4 at 160 °C for different reaction time.

(a) UV–vis absorption (red line), FL excitation spectra (green line) and emission spectra (blue line) for the C-dots. (b) FL emission spectra of C-dots with different excitation wavelengths. (c) The influence of pH values on the FL intensity of C-dots. (d) The influence of ionic strengths on the FL intensity of C-dots. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

A (a) Typical TEM image of C-dots (inset: the HRTEM image of an individual C-dots and the diameter distribution of C-dots). (b) The hydrodynamic size of C-dots. B (a) FTIR spectra of the prepared C-dots. (b) XPS spectra of C-dots. (c) High resolution C1s spectrum of C-dots. (d) High resolution N1s spectrum of C-dots.

(a) Selectivity studies of C-dots (0.0168 mg/mL) toward different analytes (10 µM). (b) The selectivity studies of C-dots toward Hg2+ (10 μM) in the absence and presence of other metal ions (10 μM). (c) Photographs of the aqueous C-dots containing various metal ions under UV light (excited at 365 nm).
(a) Fluorescent response of C-dots with different concentration of Hg2+ (0–10 μM). (b) The relationship between log (F 0/F) and the concentration of Hg2+.

(a) Effect of C-dots on cell viability of HeLa and GES-1 cells. (b) Confocal images of living HeLa and GES-1 cells which were incubated with 200 μg/mL C-dots for 4 h (left) and then incubated with 10 μM Hg2+ for 30 min at 37 °C (right).
Schematic illustration of the synthesis of C-dots and their application in label-free detection of Hg2+.
Sensitivity of various nanosensors for detection of Hg2+.
Determination of Hg2+ in real samples (n = 3).

head.ce:title: Single-step synthesis of highly photoluminescent carbon dots for rapid detection of Hg2+ with excellent sensitivity

College of Chemistry and Molecular Sciences

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: These authors contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Schematic illustration of the synthesis of C-dots and their application in label-free detection of Hg2+.

Carbon dots (C-dots) are superior in the aspects of excellent water solubility, good biocompatibility, environmentally friendliness and non-blinking fluorescence. In this work, highly photoluminescent small-size C-dots (QY = 18.8%, quinine sulfate as standard) with narrow size distribution (1.70 ± 0.21 nm) have been synthesized by using citric acid and triethylamine through hydrothermal method. The optimal excitation and emission wavelength of C-dots are 350 nm and 437 nm, respectively. And the prepared C-dots display excitation-independent behavior due to less surface defects and uniform size. Interestingly, the fluorescence of C-dots could be rapidly and selectively quenched by Hg2+ within 200 s at room temperature without further modification. Under optimal conditions, the limit of detection (LOD) was measured to be nanomolar level (2.8 nM) with a linear range of 0.05–7 μM, lower than the previous published reports. Furthermore, our results reveal that static quenching mechanism was dominated in the process in which Hg2+ coordinate with the oxygen-containing groups of C-dots to form nonfluorescent complexes. And only the addition of Hg2+ destroyed the surface defects of C-dots resulting in the fluorescent quenching. The presence of other common interfering metal ions reported in previous literature (Ag+, Cu2+, Fe3+) do not affect the surface defects, which has rarely been reported before. Besides, this sensing platform has been further successfully applied to the label free detection of Hg2+ in tap water and living cells. These conclusions demonstrate the great potential of our C-dots in selective detection of environmental and cellular Hg2+, which may achieve a lot of achievements in clinical diagnosis and other biological researches.

ce:section.ce:para: Pollution caused by heavy metal ions has become an urgent issue in the environmental fields due to their severe threat to our ecological environment and human health. One of the most dangerous heavy metal ions is mercury (II) (Hg2+) which leads great damage to our environment and health because of its highly stability [1]. And it could cause severe damage to our nervous system, brain, kidneysand even endocrine system at very low dosage [2]. Therefore, real time monitoring of Hg2+ in the environment and human body is of great significance and highly demanded. In recent years, a wide variety of analytical techniques have been established for the detection of Hg2+ with high sensitivity and accuracy, including atomic absorption and emission spectroscopy (AAS, AES) [3], inductively coupled plasma mass spectrometry (ICP-MS) [4], inductively coupled plasma atomic emission spectrometry (ICP-AES) [5]. However, expensive and sophisticated instrument and complicated pretreatment processes are usually required which limit their widespread application. Thus, development of simple methods for selective and sensitive detection of Hg2+ is urgently required. In comparison, fluorescence spectroscopy is preferred due to its high sensitivity, superior selectivity, easy operation and low cost.

Compared with traditional fluorescence (FL) based sensors, such as organic dyes [6], metal nanoclusters and semiconductor quantum dots (QDs) [7–9], carbon dots (C-dots) are superior in the aspects of excellent water solubility, good biocompatibility, environmentally friendliness and non-blinking fluorescence. Due to these excellent properties, they have emerged as candidates for a variety of practical applications, including photocatalysts, bioimaging, biosensors and chemical sensors. In the past few years, large numbers of approaches have been reported to synthesize C-dots and regulate their emission color and quantum yield, including pyrolysis, microwave, hydrothermal, laser ablation and electrochemical methods [10–12]. However, the proposed synthetic methods usually require strong acid, strong alkali, strong oxidant, organic solvent, or further modification and passivation [13]. Nitrogen doping is the most commonly used strategy to improve the poor FL quantum yield and tune the emission color of bare C-dots. And this may be derived from the fact that nitrogen doping could form a new kind of surface state and alters the energy levels once introduced into C-dots [14,15]. According to previous reports, nitrogen doping C-dots also displays negligible cytotoxicity and excellent biocompatibility which has been confirmed by both in vitro and in vivo experiments [16–18]. Compared with traditional semiconductor quantum dots, this remarkable biocompatibility makes C-dots more suitable for application in clinical diagnosis and other biological researches than semiconductor quantum dots. Keeping all these issues in mind, triethylamine is chosen which served as the surface passivating reagent as well as nitrogen source to synthesize nitrogen-doped C-dots.

Recently, Huang et al. reported the obtained C-dots for Hg2+ detection with a linear range of 0–18 μM and a detection limit of 83.5 nM [19]. Wang et al. showed a visual chemical sensor for the detection of Hg2+ by hybridizing C-dots and gold nanoclusters together [20]. A LOD of 28 nM was obtained by observing a distinct fluorescence color evolution from pink to blue after the addition of Hg2+ added into the soulution. Subsequently, biocompatible fluorescent nitrogen doped C-dots (QY = 14.2%) synthesized by critic acid and ethanolamine as precursors were applied to determine Hg2+ with a lower LOD of 19 nM, respectively [21]. So far, though many C-dots based probes for rapid detection of Hg2+ have been reported, most literature has made progress in one or two aspects in Hg2+ detection, such as improved the QYs of C-dots, increased selectivity and a lower LOD, which greatly influence their capability in practical applications. However, our sensing platform meets all the requirements of superior Hg2+ sensor, including high QYs of C-dots, excellent selectivity and low LOD in a single system which is much preferable to previous reports. Furthermore, the quenching mechanism have been studied which has rarely been considered in previous work.

Herein, a facile hydrothermal route is proposed to synthesize nitrogen-doped C-dots by using citric acid (CA) as carbon source and triethylamine (TEA) as nitrogen source as well as surface passivation agents. The as prepared C-dots possess a narrow size distribution (1.70 ± 0.21 nm) and are highly luminescent (quantum yield = 18.8%). No apparent reduction of the cell viability is observed, indicating the prepared C-dots display negligible cytotoxicity and excellent biocompatibility in vitro. C-dots emit blue light both in Hela and GES-1 cells with the negligible cytotoxicity, indicating the potential ability of C-dots for cell imaging. Interestingly, the FL intensity of C-dots could be effectively quenched by Hg2+, thus it could be used for the detection of Hg2+. Under optimal conditions, the detection of Hg2+ could be completed in 15 min with a linear range of 0.05–7 μM and a LOD of 2.8 nM. Furthermore, our results reveal that static quenching mechanism was dominated in the process in which Hg2+ coordinate with the oxygen-containing groups of C-dots to form nonfluorescent complexes. And only the addition of Hg2+ can destroy the surface defects of C-dots resulting in the fluorescent quenching. The presence of other common interfering metal ions in previous literature (Ag+, Cu2+, Fe3+) do not induce the change of surface defects of C-dots, which has rarely been reported before. Besides, this sensing system has been successfully applied to the determination of Hg2+ even in the natural water sources (tap water) and living cells. The process of synthesis and application of C-dots is displayed in the Scheme 1 .

Citric acid monohydrate (CA·H2O) and TEA were purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). All other chemicals were analytical grade and were used as received without further purification. All solutions were dissolved in deionized water with 18.0 MΩ cm (Millipore).

UV–vis absorption spectra were acquired with Cary Series UV–Vis spectrophotometer and fluorescence spectra were recorded on a Cary Eclipse fluorescence spectrophotometer at room temperature. TEM images were acquired with a JEOL 2011 transmission electron microscope at an accelerating voltage of 200 kV. The hydrodynamic diameter and zeta potential were obtained using Nano-ZS 90. X-Ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was measured with a KRATOS XSAM800 X-ray photoelectron spectrometer using Al as the exciting source. The Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectra of C-dots were carried out on a Thermo FTIR spectrophotometer. FL lifetime was measured on FLS1000.

Nitrogen-doped C-dots was prepared by a cost-effective hydrothermal method using CA and TEA as the precursors via one-pot routes. In general, 1.05 g CA·H2O was dissolved into water (25 mL) followed by adding 2.892 mL TEA. Then the mixture was transferred into 100 mL poly (tetrafluoroethylene)-lined autoclaves, and heated at 160 °C for 4 h. After the system was cooled to room temperature, the obtained light yellow solution was dialyzed against with deionized water using a 1000 Da membrane for 12 h. It should be noticed that the deionized water would be replaced every 2 h. Then the product filtered through 0.22 μm polyethersulfone (PES) water phase membrane and lyophilized.

To verify the selectivity of C-dots for Hg2+ detection, the effects of other metal ions (Mg2+, Ca2+, Zn2+, Cu2+, Ni2+, Pb2+, Al3+, Mn2+, Ag+, Cd2+ and Fe3+) on Hg2+ quantification were investigated. A series of C-dots solution (0.0168 mg/mL), tris-HCl (10 mM, pH = 9.0), metal ions (10 μM) with or without Hg2+ (10 μM) were prepared. And the FL emission spectra were then collected under 350 nm excitation, and the FL intensity at 437 nm was recorded. All experiments were performed in three times. In addition, photographs of the solutions under the exposure of 365 nm UV light were taken using a digital camera.

Initial experiments focused on exploring the optimal concentration of C-dots and the optimal pH value for Hg2+ detection. To investigate the optimal concentration of C-dots for the detection of Hg2+, the FL emission spectra of different concentrations of C-dots solution were collected under excitation by 350 nm. Then the corresponding FL emission spectra of the C-dots (0.0168 mg/mL) solution ranging from 2.0 to 12.0 were collected before and after Hg2+ (10 μM) addition.

The detection of Hg2+ experiment was performed at room temperature in C-dots aqueous solutions at pH = 9.0 (10 mM tris-HCl). Different volumes of Hg2+ solution were added into the C-dots solution, affording a series of solutions with final concentration of Hg2+ in the range of 0–10 μM (0, 0.05, 0.25, 0.5, 1.0, 1.5, 2.0, 3.0, 5.0, 7.0, 9.0 and 10.0 μM). After incubating for 15 min to equilibrate at room temperature, FL emission spectra were recorded with the excitation of 350 nm. All experiments were performed in triplicate. The LOD was calculated based on 3σ/s, where σ is the standard deviation of blank measurements and s is the slope of the calibration curve.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Sensitive detection of Hg2+ in tap water samples
ce:section.ce:para: Tap water sample were first filtered through the 0.22 μm PES water phase membrane for the detection of Hg2+. The filtered water samples were then spiked with Hg2+ to get different concentration of Hg2+ solution (0.5 μM, 1.0 μM and 1.5 μM). Then C-dots were added into each of these solutions, and content of Hg2+ in the solutions was quantified by the FL intensity. All experiments were performed in triplicate.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Assessment of cell viability and fluorescence imaging
ce:section.ce:para: Human cervical cancer cell line (HeLa) and human gastric normal cell line (GES-1) cells were cultured in Dulbecco modified Eagle medium (DMEM) containing 10% fetal bovine serum (FBS) and 1% penicillin–streptomycin at 37 °C with 5% CO2 atmosphere. The standard MTT (3-(4,5-dimethylthiazol-2-yl)-2,5diphenyltetrazolium bromide) assay was used to study the cytotoxicity of C-dots. Cells were seeded into 96-well plates at a density of 103 per well and incubated for 24 h to adhere. After that, the medium was replaced by 200 μL fresh culture medium containing different concentrations of C-dots and re-incubated for 12 h. Then the cells were washed once with PBS and 200 μL MTT (1 mg/mL) solution was added into each well for another 4 h, respectively. Then the medium was replaced with 150 μL DMSO and recorded the absorption at 490 nm by Elx800 Micropalte reader (BioTek, USA).

As for cellular imaging, 200 μg/mL C-dots solution was added into the dishes and re-incubated for 4 h after the cells adhered. Then 10 μM Hg2+ was added into the C-dots loaded cells for 30 min at 37 °C. Subsequently, the cells on glass slides were washed three times with PBS and used for confocal fluorescence imaging with the excitation of 405 nm. Then the cell was visualized by confocal fluorescence imaging (PerkinElmer UltraVIEW VoX).

Highly photoluminescent C-dots was successfully fabricated through carbonization of CA and TEA (as nitrogen sources as well as surface passivation) via a facile hydrothermal route. As reported in previous literature, the FL behavior of C-dots is the synergistic effect of various factors, including surface state, surface passivation and carbonaceous core. In order to achieve highly luminescent water-soluble C-dots, the reaction conditions are optimized by adjusting the ratio of CA to TEA, reaction temperature and reaction time.

The influence of reaction temperature and ratio of CA:TEA on the FL properties of product is given in Fig. 1 . Only when the ratio of CA:TEA was set as 1:3 and the reaction temperature was set as 160 °C, intense FL could be observed for the as prepared C-dots (Fig. 1a). The dramatical increase of the FL intensity for C-dots may be stemmed from the nitrogen doped into carbon core [22,23]. Unfortunately, when the corresponding luminescent product (CA:TEA = 1:3, 160 °C) was exposed to air for 12 h, their color turned from yellow to black, indicating that the as prepared C-dots was unstable. In order to achieve more stable product, the ratio of CA:TEA was adjusted from 1:2 to 1:4 (Fig. 1b). Although the FL intensity decreased slightly at the ratio of CA:TEA = 1:4, the prepared C-dots are more stable in aqueous solution. In addition, it should be noted that molar ratio of CA:TEA showed no significant influence on the emission wavelength of the C-dots.

Subsequently, the effect of reaction time on the carbonation process was also investigated (fixed CA:TEA = 1:4, molar ratio). Highest FL intensity can be achieved when the carbonization time was set as 4 h (Fig. 1c). As the reaction time further increased to 8 h, the FL intensity decreased a little. Further increase of reaction time to 12 h, the FL intensity drops obviously, which may be attributed to the certain degrees of aggregation of C-dots due to the longer carbonation time [23]. In addition, the reaction time also showed no effect on the FL emission wavelength of C-dots. Based on the above preliminary experimental results, highly luminescent C-dots is prepared and applied in the following assay for Hg2+ detection at molar ratio of CA/TEA = 1:4 (1.05 g CA, 2.892 mL TEA), 160 °C and 4 h.

The UV–vis absorption and FL spectra of C-dots in an aqueous solution were measured to reflect their optical properties. As shown in Fig. 2 a, the UV–vis absorption spectrum of the C-dots (red line) show two typical peaks centered at 215 nm and 334 nm, which could be attributed to the π-π* transition of aromatic sp2 domain within the C-dots and the n-π* transition of CO bond, respectively [17,24,25]. The optimal excitation and emission wavelength are centered at 350 (green line) and 437 nm (blue line). The C-dots in aqueous solution exhibits transparent color under daylight illumination while bright blue fluorescence is observed under irradiation with a 365 nm UV lamp, indicating that fluorescent C-dots have been successfully prepared (inset of Fig. 2a).

The maximum emission wavelength dependent on excitation wavelength (λex) is commonly observed in fluorescent carbon nanoparticles [26–28] while the independence of maximum fluorescence of C-dots on λex is also observed in several other studies [17,29]. Then the influence of λex on the emission wavelength of the prepared C-dots was studied accordingly. With the excited wavelength shifted from 320 nm to 390 nm, the emission peaks show no significant shift and they stay centered at 437 nm, namely excitation-independent feature is found for the as prepared C-dots (Fig. 2b). This observation indicates the good uniformity of our samples [30]. The quantum yield of the obtained C-dots is determined to be 18.8% by using the quinine sulfate (QY = 54%) as standard (Fig. S1), higher than reported recently [21,27]. The high FL intensity could be attributed to nitrogen doping because that introduction of nitrogen could form a new kind of surface state and trap electrons to facilitate the radiative recombination with much higher possibility [14,31]. C-dots display the emission at (0.17, 0.15) according to CIE color coordinates (Fig. 2c). The average FL decay lifetime was measured to be 7.38 ns (τ1 = 6.52 ns (31.67%), τ2 = 7.72 ns (68.33%), χ2 = 1.034), fitting pretty well with second order exponential decay (Fig. 5
d). At present, it is believed that the emission of C-dots mainly originates from the intrinsic emission and the surface defect emission [10,11]. The intrinsic emission usually displays strong absorption in UV region, but weak or no fluorescence while the surface defect emission usually originates from the surface non-perfect sp2 domains sites. Based on the previous reports and the results above, we think the fluorescence emission of obtained C-dots is mainly originated from surface defect emission. The approximate values of τ1 (6.52 ns) and τ2 (7.72 ns) also indicate the integrity of the surface structure of C-dots, which contributes to the independent-wavelength FL feature. Then the fluorescence stability of carbon was studied under different conditions. It is obvious that the intrinsic FL of C-dots displays a rapid increase as the pH varied from 2.0 to 6.0. When the pH values further increase from 6.0 to 13.0, the fluorescence intensity keeps almost constant (Fig. S2). In order to study the stability of C-dots under high ionic strength conditions, the FL intensity of C-dots at 437 nm is recorded in a solution containing different concentrations of KCl (aq). As illustrated in Fig. 2d, when the concentration of KCl (aq) varies from 0.2 to 2.0 M, the FL intensity of C-dots remains almost constant, indicating the great resistance of C-dots to high ionic strength environments. The FL stability of C-dots in high-salt conditions ensures its successful application for sensing Hg2+ under extreme environmental conditions.

The morphology and size distribution of C-dots was observed by TEM (Fig. 3 A(a)). The results show that the C-dots are almost spherical nanoparticles with an average size of 1.70 ± 0.21 nm. The high-resolution TEM image (Fig. 3A(a), inset) shows a well-resolved lattice fringe spacing about 0.21 nm, which can be attributed to the d-spacing of the graphene (1 0 0) planes [32,33]. So the existence of this fringe spacing suggests the presence of sp2 graphitic carbon inner core [33,34]. The statistical particle size distribution of C-dots is mainly located in the range between 1.0 and 2.5 nm (Fig. 3A(a), inset). The maximum emission wavelength independent phenomenon (Fig. 1b) may be understood through the narrow size distribution observed by TEM images, which suggests that these C-dots have more uniform size and very homogeneous surface defects on the surface [18,25]. And the hydrodynamic size of C-dots is about 10 nm (Fig. 3A(b)). X-Ray powder diffraction (XRD) of C-dots shows a broader peak at about 21.9° corresponding to the (0 0 2) interlayer spacing distance, indicating the good crystallization characteristics of C-dots (Fig. S3).

The surface chemical compositions and element states of the C-dots were investigated by FTIR and XPS spectra. As shown in Fig. 3B(a), the broad peak centered at 3431 cm−1 corresponds to the stretching vibration of OH. Two sharp peaks at 1731 and 1586 cm−1 were associated with COOH stretching vibration and asymmetric stretching vibration of COO−, respectively. Another three absorption peaks located between the region of 1175–1251 cm−1 were also appeared which is related to the stretching vibrations of COC. In addition, other several absorption peaks between 2679 and 3003 cm−1 are related with stretching vibrations of CH and CCHH. Several peaks in the two absorption regions at 1398–1478 cm−1 and 1318–1353 cm−1 can be attributed to the deformation vibrations of CH, which is usually observed in amines. Moreover, the absorption region at 665–797 cm−1 is derived from the outer of plane deformation vibration of CH in cyclic alkenes with internal double bond. Further, stretching vibrations of CN and CC skeleton vibration are observed in the absorption region at 1020–1083 cm−1 and sharp peak at 915 cm−1, respectively. These results indicate that large amounts of oxygen functional groups were formed in C-dots and partial structure of TEA were retained in the as prepared C-dots.

To further confirm the structure of the C-dots, XPS analysis was conducted. As shown in Fig. 3B(b), C-dots have three peaks centered at 286.3 eV, 401.2 eV and 532.7 eV, corresponding to the binding energies of C1s, N1s and O1s. These results indicate the obtained C-dots are composed of C, N and O elements. And data reveal that the atom ratio of the elements is 65.10%, 4.58% and 30.32%, respectively. As shown in Fig. 3B(c), the high resolution C1s spectrum is fitted into five peaks at 284.6 eV, 285.5 eV, 286.6 eV, 287.6 eV and 288.6 eV, which can be attributed to CC, CC/CN, COH, CO and COOH, respectively. Table S1 display the contents of these functional groups and indicate that the C-dots are rich in hydroxyl and carboxyl groups on the surfaces. The high resolution N1s spectrum displays two obvious peaks at 399.7 eV and 401.3 eV (Fig. 3B(d)), corresponding to pyrrolic-like N and quaternary ammonium [35–37]. The existence of quaternary ammonium further confirmed that TEA has been successfully passivated on the surface of C-dots. Meanwhile, the high resolution O1s spectrum exhibits two peaks at 531.6 eV and 533.1 eV (Fig. S4), which can be assigned to CO and COC/COH. These results got from XPS are consistent with the results of FTIR spectroscopy. Due to the large amount of OH and COOH existed on the C-dots, the as prepared C-dots possess superior water solubility, which is essential for further application in practical sample detection.

Due to their abundant carboxyl and hydroxyl groups existed on their surface, the FL intensity of various C-dots have been reported to be very sensitive to heavy metal ions, including Ag+ [38], Cu2+ [39], Au3+ [26]. Interestingly, the FL intensity of our prepared C-dots could be selectively quenched by Hg2+. As shown in Fig. 4a, compared with the blank group, serious reduction FL intensity for the C-dots are observed with the incubation of Hg2+. In contrary, the presence of other metal ions except for Hg2+ would not induce any apparent quenching of the FL intensity of C-dots. Moreover, the FL intensity can be completely quenched by the addition of 10 μM Hg2+, which can be further proved by the images of C-dots mixed with various metal ions under 365 nm (Fig. 4c). To confirm the selectivity of the C-dots toward Hg2+, we also test the FL intensity of C-dots in the presence of 10-fold interference ions as Hg2+, resulting in no significant change (Fig. S5). And other common metal interfering ions (Ag+, Cu2+, Fe3+) reported in previous literatures did not induce serious reduction of the FL intensity of C-dots. These results demonstrate the potentiality of the prepared C-dots possess high selectivity for the detection of Hg2+ in practical samples.

In order to further confirm the selectivity for detection of Hg2+, the FL changes of C-dots in the presence of Hg2+ mixed with other metal ions were studied (Fig. 4b). It is obvious that the FL of C-dots can be dramatically quenched by Hg2+, regardless of the presence of other metal ions. Moreover, compared with blank group, the quenching efficiency (%) is not affected by the presence of other metal ions. This assay also proves the feasibility and high selectively of our C-dots towards Hg2+ detection in practical complicated conditions.

Firstly, the parameters related to the detection of Hg2+ were investigated to obtain the optimal analysis performance. As shown in Fig. S6, the FL intensity versus concentration of C-dots displays a good linear relationship within the range of 0–0.168 mg/mL. In order to obtain a lower LOD, 0.0168 mg/mL C-dots was selected for the following experiments. Then the influence of pH value on the detection of Hg2+ was also studied accordingly. It is worth noting that the FL intensity of C-dots is stable in a wide range of pH values. However, in the presence of Hg2+, the FL intensity of C-dots decrease apparently to a fairly low level within the pH range varied from 2.0 to 12.0 (Fig. S7). And the biggest FL difference with or without Hg2+ occurs at the pH value of 9.0. Finally, the response time of C-dots for detecting Hg2+ was acquired by measuring the FL of C-dots containing 10 μM Hg2+ with an interval of 10 s. Most FL intensity of C-dots can be dramatically quenched in only 200 s while it reaches equilibrium within 15 min (Fig. S8). Based on the above results, the detection of Hg2+ was performed in 0.0168 mg/mL C-dots, 10 mM tris-HCl buffer (pH = 9.0) solution and 15 min incubation.

Under the optimal conditions mentioned above, we evaluated the capability of this sensing system for quantitative detection of Hg2+. As shown in Fig. 5a, the FL intensity of C-dots could be gradually quenched with the addition of Hg2+ in a typical concentration dependent manner. The FL intensity of C-dots versus the concentration of Hg2+ is displayed in Fig. 5b. With the increase of Hg2+ concentration, the FL intensity of C-dots gradually decreases and a great linearity relationship between the FL intensity and Hg2+ concentration is found in the range of 0.05–7 μM. However, the data point deviate from initial linearity when further increasing the concentration of Hg2+. So this great linear relationship mentioned above can be adopted for quantitative detection of Hg2+ with a linear equation: log (F 0/F) = 106170c + 0.0157, (R 2 = 0.999) (Fig. 5b). And the LOD is estimated to be 2.8 nM which is much lower than most previous reported values, (Table 1 ). And the LOD is also lower than the maximum level for Hg2+ to be permitted in drinking water by the European Union (5 nM) and the US Environmental Protection Agency (10 nM) [1,40].

In order to study the quenching mechanism of Hg2+ toward C-dots, we measured the FL decay lifetime of C-dots with or without Hg2+. The above data has proved that the fluorescence of C-dots is mainly consisted of two components: τ1 = 6.52 ns (31.67%) and τ2 = 7.72 ns (68.33%). As shown in Fig. 5d and Table S2, τ1 decreased rapidly as the concentration of Hg2+ increased while the proportion of τ2 decreased slightly with the addition of Hg2+, but the average FL decay lifetime remains constant. These results reveal that the quenching mechanism is static quenching in which Hg2+ interacts with C-dots to inform nonfluorescent complexes which result in the fluorescence quenching. In addition, we also studied the time-resolved FL spectra of C-dots under various common interfering metal ions (Ag+, Cu2+, Fe3+). The lifetime of τ1 is only affected by Hg2+, which further indicating the highly selectivity of our C-dots. The decrease of τ1 also confirms the formation of complexes between Hg2+ and C-dots, which lead to the change of surface-defect emission of C-dots. In addition, compared with that of bare C-dots, there is a blue shift for the absorption peak at 334 nm of C-dots and apparent decrease at 1586 cm−1 in FTIR spectra after the addition of Hg2+ , indicating static quenching mechanism was involved in the quenching process (Fig. 5c and S9) [34]. This is consistent with the previous reports that Hg2+ has strong binding affinity and fast chelating rate with the oxygen-containing groups of C-dots [41]. On the other hand, the zeta potential of C-dots in aqueous solution was measured to be −5.89 mV (Fig. S10), indicating the C-dots could electrostatically interact with Hg2+. Taken together, the fluorescence quenching of C-dots by Hg2+ is the result of static quenching mechanism. In this process, Hg2+ coordinate with the oxygen-containing groups on the surface of C-dots [34] to form nonfluorescent complexes which result in the change of surface defect emission of C-dots. Hg2+ works as an acceptor to absorb the fluorescence of C-dots via electron transfer [42,43]. The N-doping in the C-dots may effectively tune the electronic local density of C-dots and promotes the coordination interaction between oxygen functional groups on the surface of C-dots and Hg2+ [44].

Based on the above assay, this analytical system of C-dots towards Hg2+ detection possess many superior advantages (excellent selectivity, high sensitivity, fast response), which inspires us to apply the system in real samples. Firstly, the tap water samples were filtered through 0.22 μm PES water phase membrane and then spiked with different concentration of Hg2+. As shown in Table 2 , the recoveries for detecting Hg2+ in tap water samples vary in the range of 95–105%.These results suggest the applicability of the proposed strategy for label free detection of Hg2+ in real samples. More importantly, the novel strategy is much more convenient and environmentally friendly due to the absence of toxic QDs, organic dyes, and organic solvents.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Application of C-dots for cellular Hg2+ detection
ce:section.ce:para: It is widely accepted that Hg2+ is harmful to human health. Therefore, the potentiality of the C-dots for Hg2+ detection at cellular level was also studied. Firstly, the biocompatibility of C-dots was studied by MTT assay. The viability of HeLa and GES-1were investigated. When exposed to various concentrations of C-dots (0–200 μg/mL for 12 h), no apparent reduction of the cell viability for both cell lines is observed (Fig. 6 a), indicating that C-dots possess good biocompatibility and have the potential to be applied in imaging cells. Then both Hela and GES-1 are separately applied to explore the potential of C-dots to detect the intracellular Hg2+. When the cells were incubated with C-dots for 4 h, obvious blue fluorescence is observed in the cells and most C-dots are accumulated in cytosol rather than nuclei (Fig. 6b). However, as soon as the cells preincubated with C-dots were exposed to Hg2+ for 30 min, negligible intracellular fluorescence could be observed, suggesting the C-dots can detect the Hg2+ in cancer cells or normal cells without any obvious difference. These results prove the feasibility of our C-dots for cellular imaging and detection of intracellular Hg2+.

In summary, nitrogen-doped C-dots has been successfully fabricated through carbonization of CA and TEA via hydrothermal route. And the C-dots display negligible cytotoxicity and excellent biocompatibility both in Hela and GES-1 cells. Additionally, the as prepared of C-dots can serve as a highly sensitive sensor towards Hg2+ with a linear range of 0.05–7 μM and a LOD of 2.8 nM. Static quenching mechanism was dominated in this process and only the addition of Hg2+ could destroyed the surface defects of C-dots, different from other common interfering metal ions in previous literature (Ag+, Cu2+, Fe3+) which have rarely been reported before. Furthermore, the C-dots could be applied to the label free detection of Hg2+ in tap water and living cells with satisfactory results, indicating the potential value of C-dots in clinical diagnosis and other biologically related study. And it is worth to make efforts of C-dots in bioimaging, clinical diagnosis and nanomedicine, which is also the direction of our future efforts.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: We gratefully acknowledge the financial support from National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant No. 21873075, 21773178), Guangxi Scientific and Technological Development Projects (AD17195081), BAGUI Scholar Program of Guangxi Province of China, the Natural Science Foundation of Hubei Province (2018CFA090).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jcis.2019.04.088.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1
